Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe spend some time on coursework when I felt like it, usually scattered throughout the week without any real plan. I have access to good resources and tech, so when I did study, I'd just pull up whatever I needed. But organizing a consistent schedule? That wasn't really my thing this semester.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Sometimes I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then maybe do a bit when I remembered an assignment was coming up. It wasn't really tied to deadlines in a stressed way—I just kind of did things when the mood struck me. I had other stuff going on with extracurriculars that took priority most of the time.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I got a notification about something or I vaguely remembered there was a discussion post I should probably check out. Sometimes I'd hop on just to see if there was anything new or if my peers had posted something interesting. It wasn't really about studying—more like checking in to see what was happening.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, especially when the gamified tasks made things slightly more interesting. But it never became a real habit or routine. It was more sporadic—I'd engage for a bit, then drift away for a while.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually pretty decent because I could interact with other students and see different perspectives. I'm a visual learner, so when there were videos or diagrams, those helped more than walls of text. The gamified elements made some tasks feel less tedious, which I appreciated.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and longer lecture content. If something looked dry or too text-heavy, I'd just skim it or skip it entirely. I figured I could catch up later or piece things together from discussions, but that didn't always work out for exams obviously.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if it was interactive or had some kind of immediate payoff, like earning points or badges through the gamified system. If I could see progress or get feedback quickly, I was more likely to stick with it. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose? I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt way more appealing sometimes because there was no pressure. Required activities felt like obligations, which made them feel boring even if they weren't. With optional stuff, I could engage on my own terms, which weirdly made me more willing to participate in discussions and peer interactions.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs or save videos to watch later on my own time, away from the LMS. I also used YouTube tutorials and other external sites when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual learning happened outside the platform where it wouldn't be tracked.
Q10: There were times I'd be reviewing notes I'd taken during discussions with classmates or going through saved materials on my laptop. None of that shows up anywhere. I might spend an hour thinking through concepts while doing something else entirely, like during my extracurricular activities, but the LMS wouldn't know that.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then never go back to the LMS for it. I preferred having things locally so I could study without needing to be online or logged in. It was just more convenient for my scattered approach.
Q12: The middle of the semester was rough because the content got more complex and I'd already fallen behind without realizing it. I wasn't stressed exactly, but I was bored and the material wasn't grabbing me. When things feel tedious, I just naturally drift away and focus on stuff that's more engaging.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected things. My extracurriculars took up a lot of mental energy, and honestly, I prioritized those over coursework. I wasn't overwhelmed or fatigued—I just chose to put my attention elsewhere because the course wasn't holding my interest.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing while doing something else, or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. I was technically 'active' but my brain was elsewhere. It's easy to go through the motions without actually engaging.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams constantly because I figured I'd get to it eventually. The gamified tasks were easier to engage with, so I'd do those instead of the harder exam prep. I knew the exams were important, but they felt so far away until suddenly they weren't.
Q16: Deadlines would cause a small spike in my activity, but not a huge one. I'd do enough to submit assignments, even if they weren't my best work. I wasn't the type to pull all-nighters or stress about it—I'd just do what I could and accept whatever outcome.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. If I got positive feedback, cool. If it was negative, I'd think 'okay, I should do better' but then not actually change anything. It wasn't that I didn't care—it just didn't motivate me to shift my approach significantly.
Q18: The structure was pretty standard, which meant it didn't really push me to engage more. The gamified elements were a nice touch and got me participating in discussions and tasks more than I probably would have otherwise. But the overall structure didn't demand much, so I gave it minimal effort.
Q19: The gamification made engagement easier because it added some fun to otherwise boring tasks. What made it harder was when content was presented in long, text-heavy formats without visual elements. I'd just zone out. More interactive or visual content would have kept me more engaged.
Q20: I think that's probably students like me who are clicking around, participating in discussions, doing the gamified stuff, but not actually putting in the deep work for assignments and exams. You can be active and social on the platform without producing quality submissions.
Q21: That makes sense too. Someone could study efficiently offline, ace their exams, and barely touch the LMS. I'm kind of the opposite—I was active on the platform but didn't translate that into good exam performance because my engagement was surface-level.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you click on, but they can't show if you're actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking you do away from the screen, and whether your engagement is meaningful or just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's being mentally present and invested. I wasn't really engaged this semester, even though my activity numbers might suggest otherwise.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was bored and mentally checked out most of the time. It wouldn't capture that my peer interactions were more social than academic, or that I prioritized extracurriculars over coursework. The data shows activity, but not intention or quality of engagement.
